questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: She not only lost her wallet but also her phone.
options: She not only lost her wallet but also lost.
She lost not only her wallet but also.
She not only lost her wallet but also her phone.
She lost not only her wallet but also her phone.
questions.solution: She lost not only her wallet but also her phone.

options: He suffers from fever since last week.
He has been suffering from fever since last week.
He was suffering from fever since last week.
He is suffering from fever since last week.
questions.solution: He has been suffering from fever since last week.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Unsymmetrical faults:
questions.solution: LG most common. Use symmetrical components for analysis.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Applying negative feedback to an amplifier, which statement is INCORRECT?
options: It stabilises the gain against parameter changes.
questions.solution: Negative feedback reduces gain (trading it for bandwidth, lower distortion and stability).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Compensation theorem:
options: Impedance change can be modeled by series voltage source

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: PF improvement benefit:
options: Reduces I, reduces losses, reduces voltage drop, increases capacity
questions.solution: PFC: capacitor banks. KVAR = P(tanφ₁ - tanφ₂).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the series RLC circuit shown (L = 0.1 H, C = 40 µF), the resonant frequency is about:
questions.solution: At resonance XL = XC, so f₀ = 1/(2π√(LC)) = 1/(2π√(0.1×40µF)) ≈ 79.6 Hz.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Mutual inductance:
questions.solution: M = k·√(L₁L₂). k = coupling coefficient. Ideal: k=1, no leakage.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Tellegen's theorem is based fundamentally on:
questions.solution: Tellegen's theorem holds for any lumped network satisfying KVL/KCL, regardless of element nature.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Resistor 100Ω, voltage 5V. Power dissipated:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the two-wattmeter method the readings are W₁ = 1200 W and W₂ = 300 W. The total 3-phase power is:
questions.solution: Total power = W₁ + W₂ = 1200 + 300 = 1500 W (the difference relates to reactive power).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A thyristor (SCR) is turned ON by:
options: A gate pulse while forward biased
questions.solution: A gate current triggers the SCR into conduction when the anode is positive; it then latches.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: State space representation:
options: ẋ = Ax + Bu, y = Cx + Du
questions.solution: A = system matrix, B = input, C = output, D = feedforward. Controllability & observability.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Nyquist criterion:
questions.solution: Z = unstable closed-loop poles. P = unstable open-loop poles. N = encirclements of -1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: PID controller:
questions.solution: PID: P for speed, I for accuracy, D for stability. Most common industrial controller.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Steady-state error types:
options: Type 0, 1, 2 systems based on poles at origin
questions.solution: Type 0: finite K_p error. Type 1: zero SSE for step, finite for ramp.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Reciprocity theorem:
options: Current at B due to source at A = current at A if source moved to B
questions.solution: Valid for linear, bilateral, passive networks.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: RLC at f=157.60Hz (below fr=159.20Hz), R=100Ω. Circuit behavior:
questions.solution: At f<fr, XC > XL. Phase angle negative. Power factor leading.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a balanced 3-phase star system, which statement is INCORRECT?
options: Line voltage leads its nearest phase voltage by 30°.
The line current is √3 times the phase current.
Line and phase currents are equal.
The line voltage is √3 times the phase voltage.
questions.solution: In star, line and phase currents are equal; the √3 factor applies to voltages, not currents.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transformer efficiency:
questions.solution: Max efficiency at load where Cu loss = Core loss.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: RLC at f=136.50Hz (below fr=151.70Hz), R=5Ω. Circuit behavior:
questions.solution: At f<fr, XC > XL. Phase angle negative. Power factor leading.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: RLC at f=78.30Hz (above fr=71.20Hz). Circuit behavior:
questions.solution: At f>fr, XL > XC. Phase angle positive. Power factor lagging.

questions.solution: At f<fr, XC > XL. Phase angle negative. Power factor leading.

questions.solution: At f<fr, XC > XL. Phase angle negative. Power factor leading.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the series RLC circuit shown (L = 0.5 H, C = 100 µF), the resonant frequency is about:
questions.solution: At resonance XL = XC, so f₀ = 1/(2π√(LC)) = 1/(2π√(0.5×100µF)) ≈ 22.5 Hz.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: At series resonance, the impedance of an RLC circuit is:
questions.solution: XL and XC cancel, leaving only R, so impedance is minimum and resistive (current maximum).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The current-versus-frequency curve of a series RLC circuit peaks at the marked frequency f₀ ≈ 159 Hz. At this resonant frequency the power factor is:
questions.solution: At series resonance XL = XC, the impedance is purely resistive and current peaks, so the power factor is unity. (Here f₀ ≈ 159 Hz.)
